title: Assessing pasture quality for small flocks
subtitle: Reading the grass and deciding what the pasture can and cannot support.
excerpt: The carrying capacity of a pasture determines how many animals it can support without deteriorating. A smallholder managing two or three acres needs to read the grass regularly and make decisions about when to move stock on, when to rest a field, and when the pasture needs intervention. This covers simple visual assessments and the basic decisions they inform.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, pasture management for small farms. Farming Connect Wales, grassland management guides. NIAB, grass varieties guide.

content.text: Pasture quality on a small holding degrades quickly if stock pressure is not managed. The two most common causes of poor pasture on smallholdings are overgrazing in wet weather, which leads to
, and continuous year-round grazing without rest. Both can be addressed with simple management decisions; neither requires specialist equipment.

Walk the field every month. Note how much bare soil shows. Bare patches over 10% mean the ground is overgrazed or poached. Note which weeds are growing. Docks, thistles, and rushes point to poor drainage or low nutrients. Ragwort must be pulled out by the roots. And note the
across the field. Very short patches alongside ungrazed tall areas indicate selective grazing or uneven pressure.

system significantly improves pasture condition compared to continuous access. Move stock when the sward is grazed to 3 to 4 cm. Allow a rest period of at least three to four weeks before returning in summer, longer in winter. The resting paddock can be closed for hay if regrowth is sufficient.

Reducing grazing pressure during wet winter months is the single most effective action for improving pasture the following spring. Sacrifice one small paddock for winter standing if stock must remain outside, and rest the main pasture. Poached ground in winter takes a full growing season to recover.
